MLB Prediction System Notes

How The MLB Board Works

Loading run details...

Back to prediction bounties

Board Snapshot

This page summarizes the current MLB high-precision board. Instead of a wide “maybe playable” pool, the selector trims down to rows with stronger edge, fresher history, and better estimated hit probability.

By Target

By Direction

Pipeline Overview

The current MLB board is built from the raw daily prediction pool, then reranked by a stricter hit-focused selector. Right now the strongest committed MLB signal in the repo is the hit market, so the board intentionally narrows rather than pretending every target is equally production-ready.

Candidate rows are filtered on modeled support, edge size, history depth, recency, and push exposure. The board then applies concentration caps so one player, team, or game script cannot dominate the final set.

How A Play Is Built

  1. The model pool produces candidate rows for supported MLB stat targets.
  2. The selector estimates directional hit probability from the model mean and the posted line.
  3. Weak edge, stale-history, low-support, and high-push rows are filtered out.
  4. Remaining plays are ranked by a precision score that favors safer hit-focused profiles.
  5. Player, game, and team caps keep the published board from overconcentrating risk.

What The Metrics Mean

  • Estimated Hit Rate: directional win probability from the current model mean versus line.
  • Graded Hit Rate: hit rate after removing push probability from the denominator.
  • Abs Edge: absolute difference between prediction and market line.
  • Precision Score: a composite ranking score used to tighten the board.
  • Supported Rows: candidate rows with a non-baseline modeled signal the selector can trust.
